resharedPost.text: DeepSeek R1 now joins the 1,800+ models in the model catalog in Azure AI Foundry and GitHub, offering scalability, enterprise readiness, and cost-efficient AI capabilities for developers and businesses alike. With DeepSeek, you can now access the latest AI model with minimal infrastructure investment. Learn more: https://msft.it/6045UVP9v #AzureAIFoundry

comments.text: 1. Is an extremely impressive  model the team deserves a ton of credit, the chain of thought inner monologue gives me a chuckle sometimes. 2. DeepSeek deserves a ton of credit for open sourcing this, and teaching the world how they did it. 3. It’s not all roses, ask it about Taiwan  you will notice it does not even *think*, I don’t think has anything to do with DeepSeek rather what they are forced to do. 4. It’s open, every knob in tuning is being turned, yes you can run it completely locally no internet. That does not remove the fact It is concerning there is that kind of political propaganda by an AI already being pushed into models by governments. 5. The comparisons in absolute to open ai are silly, it’s not a zero sum game, as far as the 01 tier, deep seek deserves to be close to the top. Maybe they have an 03 answer, maybe they don’t. The only facts we know is 03 is in the hands of real world people, and it makes 01 look like gpt3. At this point nothing but benefit for the consumer side, let the games begin!
A great and quick step! Making it easier to deploy enterprise-ready AI models with minimal infrastructure costs.

Great initiative by Microsoft Azure to expand the AI Foundry with DeepSeek R1! It’s exciting to see how this aligns with the growing demand for scalable and cost-efficient AI solutions. On a related note,Amazon Web Services (AWS)’s integration of DeepSeek into Bedrock and SageMaker is also pushing boundaries, particularly with its serverless capabilities for V3 and R1 models. It would be interesting to compare how both platforms handle model accessibility, scalability, and enterprise readiness. AI innovation across ecosystems is certainly raising the bar for developers and businesses alike!

posts.text:
Don’t fall for false DeepSeek R1 news! Deepseek R1 made it to mainstream, but there are so many wrong takes and false information. ℹ️

> No, the training didn’t cost only ~$6M dollars; the compute for the base model (no RL) was equivalent to GPU hours worth $5.5M, excluding ablations, smaller runs, data generation, and any of DeepSeek R1 training.

> No, it's not a side project (maybe started as one). DeepSeek is backed and owned by High-Flyer, a Chinese hedge fund; in 2020, they managed assets of over 7 billion dollars, and their talent includes Olympic medalists in mathematics, physics, and informatics.

> No, they don’t just have a few GPUs. They have ~50k GPUs.

> The real Deepseek R1 is 671B MoE model that needs > 16x 80GB Memory (16x H100s).

> Yes, the Deepseek R1 671B is really good! And they do great open source and science work > 2 years already

> There are 6 “distilled” versions. They are fine-tuned Qwen and Llama on 800k samples, (NO RL). Thats not “r1”. The smallest one with 1.5B (Yes, you can run locally, but it’s not near R1).

> Yes, the hosted version on chat(.)deepseek(.)com might use your data to train new models (as per ToS of DS).

> Yes, Open Science and Source will benefit everyone in the long term! Hugging Face is working on a fully open reproduction pipeline

If you are curious how Deepseek R1 trained, I wrote a blog: https://lnkd.in/eKnT7bHC

The model is producing good results comparable to the commercial closed source models but its security risks are higher. I wrote a post about its rank in the AI Trust Score and it ranks in the middle - while low in security but comparable in other risks like hallucination propensity, bias, toxicity and psychological safety. In the end businesses have to look at the overall risk and ROI and with the Tumeryk  AI Trust Score Manager (http://tumeryk.com) they can operate within the Trust Zone to address the risks.

comments.text: I appreciate the information you shared about Deepseek R1, but I have to respectfully disagree with some of your points. While the training cost may not be exactly $6M, it's still a significant amount of money that not everyone has access to. Additionally, while Deepseek may be backed by a hedge fund, that doesn't necessarily mean it's not a side project for the individuals working on it. And while they may have a large number of GPUs, that doesn't necessarily mean they are using them all for Deepseek R1.
